structured_choices.data_source: 100,000+ customer reviews from e-commerce platform over 24 months. Each record includes: timestamped text review, associated product image(s), post-purchase satisfaction survey score (continuous, 1–5 or 1–10 scale), product category, purchase date, price tier, and promotional flag. Data collected from production environment; no synthetic data used.
structured_choices.label_definition: Customer satisfaction score obtained from post-purchase survey administered within 14 days of delivery. Scores are continuous (e.g., 1–5 on Likert scale or 0–100 percentage). No binning or discretization performed; regression task preserves ordinal structure. Missing labels are excluded (expect <5% loss). Label distribution and temporal trends are profiled to detect seasonality or drift.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal split with product-category grouping to prevent leakage and control for confounds. (1) Identify review collection date and group by month. (2) Stratify by product category (e.g., electronics, apparel, home goods) to ensure each category is represented proportionally in train/val/test. (3) Allocate: months 1–16 (first 16 months) to training (60% of total records after stratification), months 17–20 to validation (20% of records), months 21–24 to test (20% of records). This temporal stratification ensures: (a) no future information enters training, (b) seasonal effects are partly controlled by including full seasonal cycles in training, (c) within-category imbalance is preserved in each fold, enabling fair evaluation of model calibration across satisfaction levels.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Text tokenizer, image resizer/normalizer, and any statistical transformers (e.g., scaling for regression targets) are fit exclusively on the training set. Validation and test sets are preprocessed using train-fitted transformers. Fitted objects (tokenizer vocabulary, image mean/std, label distribution statistics) are versioned and locked before validation/test evaluation to prevent data leakage.
structured_choices.features: Text modality: (1) Token embeddings from a pretrained language model (e.g., BERT, DistilBERT) fine-tuned on training data or frozen and used as feature extractor; extract [CLS] token and penultimate layer representations. (2) Lexical features: review length, average token length, presence of negation words, sentiment keywords (curated or from lexicon). Image modality: (1) Deep features from a pretrained CNN (e.g., ResNet-50, EfficientNet) pretrained on ImageNet; extract final pooled layer (2048-d or equivalent). (2) Hand-crafted image features: color histogram statistics, brightness, contrast (to capture product presentation quality). Metadata: product category (one-hot or embedding), price tier (binned or normalized), promotional flag, day-of-week, and month (cyclical encoding for seasonality). All features are concatenated into a single input vector for the multimodal model. No target encoding or label leakage.
structured_choices.model: Multimodal neural network with separate embedding branches: (1) Text branch: BERT encoder → fully connected layers (256 → 128 units). (2) Image branch: ResNet-50 CNN → fully connected layers (256 → 128 units). (3) Metadata branch: embedding layer for categorical features → fully connected layer (64 units). (4) Fusion layer: concatenate outputs from all three branches → dense layers (256 → 128 → 64 units) with ReLU activation and dropout (0.3). (5) Output layer: single neuron with linear activation for continuous satisfaction score prediction. Total parameters ~10–15M. Trained with Adam optimizer (lr=1e-4), MSE loss. Early stopping based on validation MAE (monitored throughout training).
structured_choices.baseline: Purely text-based model using identical architecture but image and metadata branches removed. The text branch is identical to the multimodal model (BERT → FC 256 → 128). To ensure fair comparison: (1) Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over learning rates, dropout, layer sizes, batch size, performed on validation set). (2) Both use identical preprocessing (fitted on training data). (3) Both trained for equal wall-clock time or number of epochs. (4) Baseline is evaluated on the same test set under the same conditions. This ensures the performance gain (if any) is attributable to multimodal fusion, not differential tuning or compute.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on test set. Justification: (1) Stakeholders care about prediction accuracy for satisfaction scores; MAE directly quantifies average prediction error in units of the satisfaction scale, making it interpretable (e.g., 'average error is 0.3 points on a 5-point scale'). (2) MAE is robust to skewed distributions (important given high-satisfaction bias) and does not overemphasize large errors like MSE. (3) For regression with continuous targets, MAE is appropriate and operationally meaningful for customer segmentation and targeted strategies (e.g., predicted scores below 3.0 trigger outreach). (4) No threshold sensitivity; prediction works across the full scale.
secondary_metrics: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — captures sensitivity to large errors, relevant if large mispredictions are costly.
Pearson correlation coefficient (ρ) between predicted and actual satisfaction — assesses ranking quality; relevant for prioritizing products for improvement.
Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) — normalized error, useful if absolute error varies by satisfaction level.
Prediction bias (mean residual) — checks for systematic over/under-prediction; important for calibration if model is used in threshold-based decisions.

Performance stratified by satisfaction score quintile — examines whether the model performs equally well across high, medium, and low satisfaction; unequal performance suggests the model is biased toward predicting high scores.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 17–20, 20% of records) is used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Grid search over hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.4}, batch size ∈ {32, 64, 128}) is performed on the validation set. The model checkpoint with lowest validation MAE is retained. Test set is held out and never touched during model selection or hyperparameter optimization.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set (months 21–24) is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete. No additional tuning or model re-training is permitted after test evaluation. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap resampling of test predictions). No test set leakage into training or validation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confounds (seasonality, promotional effects, customer bias) are addressed as follows: (1) Temporal stratification ensures training spans a full seasonal cycle (16 months), enabling the model to learn seasonal patterns rather than be misled by them. (2) Promotional flag and month (cyclical encoding) are included as explicit features, allowing the model to adjust predictions when promotions are active. (3) Product category stratification ensures the test set includes proportional representation of all product types; if satisfaction differs by category, this is captured in the stratified sample. (4) Residual analysis on test set stratified by season and promotion flag checks for systematic bias; if the model performs worse in January or during promotions, this indicates uncontrolled confounding. (5) No causal claim is made; the experiment establishes predictive superiority under the observed distribution, not causality. The multimodal model may outperform text-only due to image features capturing product quality, customer expectations, or presentation — the mechanism is not identified.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal vs. Text-Only Sentiment Analysis for Customer Satisfaction Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 100,000+ customer reviews collected over 24 months from the production e-commerce platform. Each record comprises: timestamped text review, associated product image(s), continuous customer satisfaction score (obtained from post-purchase survey within 14 days of delivery), product category, purchase date, price tier, and promotional status. The continuous satisfaction score is the regression target; no discretization is performed to preserve ordinal information. Records with missing labels are excluded (expected loss <5%). A preliminary data audit is conducted to profile the label distribution, detect temporal drift in satisfaction trends, and identify any systematic missing patterns by product category or season.

### 2. Split Strategy

A stratified temporal split is used to prevent leakage and control for known confounds (seasonality, promotions). The procedure is:

1. Group records by review collection month to establish temporal order.
2. Stratify by product category (e.g., electronics, apparel, home) to ensure proportional representation across splits.
3. Allocate months 1–16 to training (≈60% of stratified records), months 17–20 to validation (≈20%), and months 21–24 to test (≈20%).

**Justification:** Temporal stratification prevents the model from learning future trends and ensures seasonal cycles are represented in training. Within-category stratification preserves the distribution of satisfaction across product types and mitigates category-specific confounds. The test set represents held-out future observations, a fair proxy for deployment performance.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Text modality:** Token embeddings from a pretrained BERT model (fine-tuned on the training corpus or frozen) plus lexical features (review length, negation presence, sentiment keywords). Extract [CLS] token and penultimate layer representations (768-dimensional) as the primary text representation.

**Image modality:** Deep features from ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet (final pooled layer, 2048-dimensional). Additionally, compute hand-crafted statistics: color histogram, brightness, and contrast to capture product presentation quality.

**Metadata:** Product category (one-hot or learned embedding), price tier (binned or normalized), promotional flag, day-of-week, and month (encoded cyclically to capture seasonality without artificial discontinuity at year boundary).

All three modality branches are concatenated into a unified feature vector. Preprocessing (tokenization, image resizing, normalization) is fit exclusively on training data and applied consistently to validation and test sets. No target encoding or label information enters the feature engineering pipeline.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Multimodal model:** A neural network with three parallel embedding branches (text, image, metadata) that merge via concatenation and pass through fully connected layers (256 → 128 → 64 units with ReLU and dropout 0.3) before a linear output layer for regression. Trained with Adam optimizer (lr=1e-4) and MSE loss; early stopping monitors validation MAE.

**Baseline:** Purely text-based model using the identical architecture but without image and metadata branches. The text embedding branch is preserved unchanged. To ensure fair comparison, both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning (grid search over learning rate, dropout, batch size on validation data) and are trained under identical conditions (same optimizer, loss, early stopping criterion, compute budget).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the held-out test set. MAE is appropriate because: (1) it is directly interpretable in units of the satisfaction scale (e.g., 0.3-point error on a 5-point scale), (2) it is robust to the known high-satisfaction bias in the data, (3) it aligns with the stakeholder objective to predict satisfaction for targeted retention strategies, and (4) it avoids artificial emphasis on large errors.

**Secondary metrics:**
- RMSE — sensitivity to large errors.
- Pearson correlation — ranking quality for product improvement prioritization.
- Prediction bias (mean residual) — checks for systematic under/over-prediction.
- Stratified MAE by satisfaction quintile — assesses whether the model is biased toward high scores or performs uniformly.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 17–20) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. A grid search explores combinations of learning rate (1e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4), dropout (0.2, 0.3, 0.4), and batch size (32, 64, 128). The model checkpoint with the lowest validation MAE is selected. The test set is held out completely during this phase and evaluated exactly once after model selection concludes. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped) and no further model adjustment is permitted.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Known confounds (seasonality, promotions, category-specific biases) are addressed through: (1) temporal stratification ensuring the training set spans a full seasonal cycle, (2) explicit inclusion of month (cyclical), promotion flag, and category as features, (3) post-hoc residual analysis stratified by season and promotion to detect any remaining systematic bias. The experiment establishes predictive superiority of the multimodal model on the observed distribution; no causal mechanism is inferred. If multimodal performance is superior, the driver could be image features capturing product quality, customer expectations, or presentation — the design does not distinguish these mechanisms.
